article.title: SpacemiT Demos a 'Usably Quick' RISC-V Mini Desktop on Its 16-Core K3, as the Open Architecture Inches Toward Everyday Computing
article.summary: At the Ubuntu Summit, SpacemiT showed its 16-core Key Stone K3 running Ubuntu responsively, with a Banana Pi board priced at roughly twice a Raspberry Pi 5.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

The open RISC-V instruction set, long confined to microcontrollers and developer boards, is edging closer to being usable as an everyday desktop. At the Ubuntu Summit, Chinese chip designer SpacemiT demonstrated its new Key Stone K3 system-on-chip running Ubuntu smoothly enough that, according to [The Register](https://www.theregister.com/systems/2026/06/10/spacemit-shows-off-usably-quick-risc-v-mini-desktop/5252935), "Video played smoothly, and what's more, the machine stayed responsive while it was doing so." The Register described the K3 as "a fairly hefty 16-core device" and noted the hands-on was a marked improvement over previous RISC-V kit shown at the event.

## What We Know

The Key Stone K3 packs 16 CPU cores, split between eight of SpacemiT's own X100 cores running at up to 2.4 GHz and eight A100 "AI cores," as reported by [The Register](https://www.theregister.com/systems/2026/06/10/spacemit-shows-off-usably-quick-risc-v-mini-desktop/5252935). The eight A100 cores carry 1024-bit RVV 1.0 vector support and deliver up to 60 TOPS at INT4 precision, according to [Lunar Computer](https://lunar.computer/spacemit-k3-brings-rk3588-class-risc-v-20260511).

The CPU cores meet the RVA23 specification, which The Register calls significant: the RISC-V association described RVA23 as "a major milestone" that "delivers both full vector-math acceleration as well as hypervisor capabilities," per [The Register](https://www.theregister.com/systems/2026/06/10/spacemit-shows-off-usably-quick-risc-v-mini-desktop/5252935). Canonical considers the profile significant too and announced support for the new SoC in February 2026, the same report notes.

On the software side, Ubuntu is fully supported, and SpacemiT also backs other distributions, both Western and Chinese, notably its own Bianbu OS, which appears to be based on Ubuntu and LXQt, according to [The Register](https://www.theregister.com/systems/2026/06/10/spacemit-shows-off-usably-quick-risc-v-mini-desktop/5252935). The K3 SoC and its Pico-ITX board landed initial mainline support in Linux 7.0, as reported by [Lunar Computer](https://lunar.computer/spacemit-k3-brings-rk3588-class-risc-v-20260511) — the kernel release that [previously shipped with stable Rust support](/article/2026-04/14-linux-70-released-with-stable-rust-support-self-healing-xfs-and-sched_ext-as-torvalds-reflects-on-ai-driven-bug-discovery).

The surrounding hardware reflects desktop ambitions rather than microcontroller roots. The K3 boards offer 8GB, 16GB, or 32GB of LPDDR5 at 6400 MT/s and 128GB or 256GB of UFS 2.2 storage, with a 10GbE SFP+ cage driven by a Realtek RTL8127 controller, according to [CNX Software](https://www.cnx-software.com/2026/05/11/rva23-pico-itx-sbc-spacemit-k3-octa-core-risc-v-ai-soc-up-to-32gb-ram-256gb-ufs/). Lunar Computer adds that the Pico-ITX board includes an M.2 Key-M 2280 slot for PCIe Gen3 x4 NVMe drives, according to [Lunar Computer](https://lunar.computer/spacemit-k3-brings-rk3588-class-risc-v-20260511).

On raw performance, the K3 reaches 130 KDMIPS — similar to the Rockchip RK3588 — with multi-core results on par with the RK3588 and single-core numbers slightly below a Raspberry Pi 5, according to [CNX Software](https://www.cnx-software.com/2026/05/11/rva23-pico-itx-sbc-spacemit-k3-octa-core-risc-v-ai-soc-up-to-32gb-ram-256gb-ufs/).

## Price and Availability

Banana Pi launched the K3 Pico-ITX board in May, according to [The Register](https://www.theregister.com/systems/2026/06/10/spacemit-shows-off-usably-quick-risc-v-mini-desktop/5252935). The Register found an 8GB RAM version on AliExpress for slightly under £300 (US $400), though the listing put the recommended retail price at £595 (just under $800). That is roughly twice the price of a Raspberry Pi 5 with 8GB of RAM, which sells for £168 in the UK or $175 in the US — albeit for a much higher-spec device, the report notes. CNX Software lists the bare Pico-ITX SBC starting at $299 and the CoM260 system-on-module devkit at $309, according to [CNX Software](https://www.cnx-software.com/2026/05/11/rva23-pico-itx-sbc-spacemit-k3-octa-core-risc-v-ai-soc-up-to-32gb-ram-256gb-ufs/).

## What We Don't Know

The demonstration was a hands-on impression rather than a controlled benchmark suite, and The Register's account does not quantify the K3's desktop responsiveness beyond the smooth-video observation. The pricing picture is also unsettled: the gap between the sub-$400 AliExpress listing and the near-$800 recommended retail price leaves the device's real-world cost uncertain. Broader questions — software maturity across the RISC-V application ecosystem, and how the K3 compares against Arm desktop boards beyond the RK3588 and Raspberry Pi 5 reference points — remain open in the cited reporting.

## Analysis

The significance here is less about the K3's absolute speed than about the trajectory. A RISC-V mini PC that plays video smoothly and stays responsive under Ubuntu, with mainline kernel support and RVA23's vector and hypervisor features, represents a different class of usability than the developer boards that defined the architecture's first decade. It also extends RISC-V's recent push beyond embedded niches — a trend visible in the architecture's move into [AI data-center infrastructure backed by major investors](/article/2026-04/16-sifive-raises-400-million-at-365-billion-valuation-as-nvidia-and-apollo-back-risc-vs-push-into-ai-data-centers). The price premium over an Arm-based Raspberry Pi 5 underscores that open silicon still carries a cost penalty at the consumer edge, but a board that is merely twice the price of a Pi 5 — rather than an order of magnitude more — is a narrower gap than RISC-V desktop hardware has historically commanded.
